sentences.text: Call me Nora.
Nora Danzig.
N-O-R-A.
Danzig, D-A-N-Z-I-G.
Nora Braunschweiger.
B-R-A-U-N-S-C-H-W-E-I-G-E-R.
No, except my Hebrew name.
Naomi.
N-A-O-M-I.
The 3rd of April, 1930.
My age now is 66.
I was born in Hosbach, by Aschaffenburg, which is near Frankfurt and Mainz.
Germany.
Hosbach, it is H-O, umlaut, S-B-A-C-H.
Well, I-- I remember the first part of my life was a very happy life.
We lived in a small village.
There didn't seem to be much anti-Semitism.
I come from a very happy, close family, traditionally Jewish.
We kept all the Jewish holidays and Shabbat.
I had a lovely childhood because my grandfather was a dealer of cattle, so I lived amongst a small amount of animals.
And we had some fields and a very pretty little garden.
So, and I had-- it was a great joy.
We mixed freely with non-Jews, who were friends, and treated us the same as they.
[COUGHING] Although we kept all the Jewish festivals, I also used to enjoy my neighbor's-- our neighbor's Christmas and Easter.
So it was a very pleasant, happy childhood.
I went to school-- to a village school because there was nothing nearby.
The nearest town was Frankfurt.
And we were-- both my sister and I were far too young to go to Frankfurt.
The anti-Semitism that I remember started perhaps in '37, but mainly from the next village, which was Goldbach.
They-- my village, they were mainly Roman Catholics, staunch Roman Catholics.
But the next village was much more mixed.
And they used to come into our village singing these awful songs and throwing stones at windows.
And my first real memory of Nazism was when my father was taken away.
Because they came into the village singing these songs and he went out and probably told them to get off in no uncertain terms.
And the next day he was taken away.
But he came back after some time.
I can't remember the length of time, that I remember I was the first one to see him.
And I screamed, papa is here papa is here.
And my mother couldn't believe it and she came running out of the house.
And we were then left alone.
But I think it became too difficult to live there.
And I think the end of it, '38, we moved to Frankfurt.
Well, I'm told, bearing in mind I was very young, and I was-- I'm told that there were about six Jewish families, and all called Lowenthal, which was my mother's maiden name.
I can't really tell you much about them, except we used to have a good community relationship with each other, especially on the Sabbath.
We would visit each other's homes and suchlike.
I remember one little Jewish girl called Steffi, but I really remember very little about the people.
I can't even remember school days.
Although I have some pictures of my early school days, I don't remember anything about it.
Well, there was Pesach.

And I remember when Pesach was over, the matzahs that remained we used to make into parcels, and we used to take to our non-Jewish friends, who used to seem to like having matzahs for some unknown reason.
And I remember Hanukkah, that was very important, because the table was laden with presents.
I was rather, I suppose, spoiled in that respect because my sister wasn't interested in toys.
So I used to have all hers as well.
What other festivals can I remember?
I know on Saturdays we used to go to synagogue, which was in Goldbach.
We didn't have a local synagogue.
It was only a matter of probably a mile's walk at the most.
And Hebrew lessons were, I think, on Sundays.
And the teacher would take it in turns to come to the various Jewish houses and meet together in that particular house for our Hebrew lessons.
Yes, I would say they were.
Not ultra orthodox, but we certainly kept everything.
We kept milchik and fleishik, and all the festivals, and Sabbath was kept-- the traditional sort of Jewish family.
My father's profession was, in German, ein Kaufman.
I can't think of the English equivalent.
A merchant, yes, yes.
And my grandfather was a dealer of-- officially, a dealer of cattle.
Although I don't think he did very much because he was not so young anymore.
My grandmother, I remember with great joy.
She was probably one of my favorite people.
And she died, thank goodness, in 1936.
And she was a very, very popular figure.
I remember, I think the whole village came to her funeral, not Jew and non-Jew alike, because she was a very good and generous person.
And everybody, even-- I've returned to the village since, they still talk about her.
And she saved me from many a good hiding, so I loved her dearly.
The relationships, as I think I said previously, it was very good between Jew and non-Jew in the village, certainly until '38.
I think we left Hosbach probably in '38 or very late '37.
I don't know exactly.
Yes, I think it was just the village school.
That's all I can remember.
There was no Jewish school.
So, but we had our Jewish lessons, as I mentioned on Sundays.
Nothing.
I can't remember anything.
I think my memory, you see, hasn't been jogged by anyone because I lost all my family.
So I haven't really had anyone to say to me, do you remember so-and-so.
So I can't remember anything about the school at all.
One particular school friend who remembers me clearly found me after the war, searched me out.
And I'm now still in correspondence with her.
And I see her occasionally and her family.
I can't remember anything.
Nothing, I can't remember a teacher.
I can't remember anything.
My sister was at the same school, yes.
My sister's name is Inge Paula, or Paula in English.
My sister was three years older than I am.
[COUGHING] Excuse me.
We lived with my grandparents and, of course, my mother and father and my sister and I, in a real old village house, which was connected by the-- on one side was-- was our house.
And I understand there was another house behind, which was let to some people.
And on the opposite side in the front on the main street was a little shop which my father owned, selling materials.
And behind it was a cow shed, where they were-- I don't know-- three, four cows.
And that I remember a lot, because we used to get into terrible trouble with my grandfather, because we used to play in the hay, which was above the cow shed.
And you mustn't trample hay down for the cows.
But of course we loved it.
And we used to play up there.
And we used to do lots of naughty things, my neighbor's son and I.
That I can remember.
And my grandfather was always chasing us, trying to get us out of this hay loft.
No, the neighbor was not Jewish.
I think they, as far as I know, they rented the house called the Hinter House.
I think they rented it.
And in fact, all I remember is the boy who I used to play with, who was older, a bit older than I am.
His name was Albert.
And I recently saw him for the first time since probably 1937.
He now lives in America.
And that is more or less the only person I can remember from that period of my life, apart from my family, of course.
Well--
I mean, I was only-- don't forget, I was only 7, so it was not discussed with me very much.
All I can remember is that I think-- I think we, that we were sort of half-forced to sell our house and our land and our property.
And the next I knew we moved to Frankfurt and Mainz.
We moved to the Jewish area.
I remember the address.
It was [GERMAN].
And the Jewish school, called Philanthropin, which I believe is very famous, because there were only two Jewish schools in Frankfurt.
And the small block of flats that we lived in was built onto the school.
So I never had far to go to the school.
The main thing I remember is that, more or less, our whole family from my mother's side seem to live in this little block of flats.
Perhaps there were four or five flats in this house.
And everybody, as many people as possible, squashed into each flat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do remember Kristallnacht.
I don't remember much else because I don't think I was allowed to wander the streets on my own.
And I never had far to go to school because it was literally next door.
My sister and I went to that school.
I don't think I was really told.
I just know we moved, that's all I seem to remember.
I don't think my parents told us-- told me, anyway.
Perhaps-- perhaps Inge knew more because she was a bit older than me.
I probably was quite childish, seven and eight years old, because I was the little one of the family.
With other relatives.
They were-- my mother was one of four sisters.
I believe they all lived in this little block of flats.
And the husbands that were remaining lived there and their children.
But I don't honestly remember too much who lived there.
My grandfather lived with us, definitely.
And I think an uncle lived in our flat.
I think my grandfather, my uncle, my mother, and my father, my sister and I, lived in that flat.
And I think there was even somebody else, but I don't know who it was.
I can't remember that.
And the rest were spread out in the rest of the--
Oh no, they all moved because of the, because of having to-- You see, my whole family lived in villages.

There were pockets of Jews in these little villages all around Frankfurt, and all quite-- I don't know how they managed to survive there, because there were small pockets of Jews that were very quite traditional Jews.
And I know I used to visit my aunts.
Perhaps for Pesach, I would go there, or one of the other Jewish festivals.
They were all living within probably a 30 mile radius of each other, because nobody moved too far in those days.
And I do remember visiting various aunts and uncles.
But then they all had to leave.
It was getting too difficult to live in these small villages.
I don't know.
I mean, I can trace the Braunschweiger side of my family, who came from [GERMAN], living there from about 1700.
And my mother's side, the Lowenthals, living in Hosbach also.
I've got papers that go back to about 1700.
And if, I have recently-- well, not recently, about four years ago-- come to the cemetery that served about six villages.
And it's quite carefully looked after, although obviously quite a few gravestones have disappeared.
But many date back to 1600, from these, all from these surrounding villages.
I don't know-- I don't know how they got there or why they live there.
But bear in mind that I think Jews weren't allowed to do very much.
They weren't allowed to do professional things.
They were allowed to be moneylenders.
They were allowed to be keepers of animals, and such like tailors, and so on.
But they weren't allowed to be professionals, I think.
And I often wonder why my father's name was Braunschweiger.
Maybe his great great grandfather came from Braunschweig.
Because they didn't have surnames, did they?
I think-- I think I'm right in saying.
Let's go back to 1938.
What do you remember, what was your day like in Frankfurt?
What was my day like?
All I can remember is going to school.
And I think the day was spent in school.
I don't think I'd go home for lunch, and then going home in the evenings.
I can't remember too much apart from that.
I think you know that was the time when they weren't allowed to work anymore.
You know, they smashed up the synagogues on Kristallnacht, and all the-- and a lot of houses were attacked.
Our place wasn't.
And I've been told the reason for it was because they wanted the Philanthropin kept whole.
It was guarded on Kristallnacht because they used it later as a Sammelplatz.
They collected the Jews and put them there.
That was after I left, though.
And it still stands today.
I believe it's now an old-age home, a Jewish old-age home.
I can remember-- I can remember the tinkling of glass, the breaking of glass.
Of course, I didn't-- we didn't leave the flat.
My father must have-- we must have known a bit what was going to happen because he disappeared, he hid.
But my uncle, who was ill, was in bed.
Excuse me.
[COUGHING] And why I had remembered it, I suppose, was because I had my little case packed.
And my mother said to me, will you give your little case to your uncle.
They're going to take him away.
And that was, I didn't really want to give it to him, but I did.
I gave him my little case, which was packed with little bits of food and so on, ready for my own journey.
And I don't remember ever seeing him again.
They took him out, they dragged him out of his bed and took him away.
My father, luckily, as I said, had hidden.
And he appeared the next day.
So I-- he still was with us until, certainly until I left, my sister and I left, which was in January 1939.
Apart from that, I really can't remember anything.
My mother and my sister and this uncle.
And then my father appeared, I think, the next day or the day after.
I don't remember exactly when.
I don't know.
I can't remember that.
I suppose I must have then.
And because they didn't close the Jewish schools.
And Jewish children could only go to Jewish schools.
We couldn't go to a non-Jewish school, which, it didn't apply because we were going to a Jewish school anyway.
Well, Kristallnacht, when was Kristallnacht, I can't remember.
Was it in September?
I can't remember.
In November.
November the 6th, I believe, or 9th? 9th.
Well, so November the 9th.
I left in January, so there wasn't much time then.
I just remember-- I remember crying bitterly because I didn't want to go.
And my parents-- my sister was going anyway.
She-- and then the people who were taking her agreed to take me as well.
And I remember I didn't want to go, but they bribed me.
We had-- they had papers, which were sent by an uncle that was living in South America.
And then we were all supposed to go to South America, all of us.
But unfortunately, it didn't happen.
They couldn't get the final permission to leave.
So, but I didn't know that of course.
But they said, we are coming to collect you.
Within three months, we'll collect you from London and we'll all go to South America together.
And on this condition, I came to England.
And I remember leaving.
It must have been in that period, I suppose.
Because I know my sister was going.
But I wasn't, I absolutely refused.
Would you excuse me.
I need a drink of water.
[COUGHING] I-- I just didn't want to leave my parents.
But as I said, they-- they must have seen how bad things were.
I think they realized this was it.
So they persuaded me on the strength of them coming to collect me-- or us, I should say-- within three months.
In fact, I don't know if all children-- because I can came on a children's transport.
And we only had permission to stay, I believe, for six months in England.
But of course, this was altered.
And I-- I believed them.
So on these grounds, I came to England.
Well, we left on January-- I don't, it must have been the 17th, I think, because we arrived in England on the 18th.
And I think it was an overnight journey.
I remember being taken with my sister to the Frankfurt station, and we were put on a train.
And that's the last time I saw my parents.
We traveled.
I remember traveling through Holland, where food was passed to us through the window of the train.
And it was the first time I saw tea with milk.
And none of us knew what it was because we'd never seen this before.
But that-- that stuck in my mind.
And the next thing I remember on the boat, which must have been an overnight journey.
It was an overnight journey because we were in bunks.
And I remember putting a little chain next to me in the container, in case you were ill in the night.
And I had this chain between two containers.
And I forgot it when they were told to get up and leave.
And I remembered it afterwards and I rushed back and I still found the chain, which I brought to England.
And the next thing I remember is London, coming out on a train to London.
I don't remember the arrival of the boat, but I remember arriving in London because of the big red buses, which we'd, of course, never seen before.
And we were all taken to a hall where we awaited to be picked up by the various people that had agreed to take us.
We weren't allowed-- we were allowed to take what a child could carry, which was very little-- one suitcase and one little case.
That a child couldn't carry much more than that.
So that is what I carried.
But I really-- my biggest memory were leibchen.
Do you know what ein leibchen is?
It's a liberty bodice, which was the most strange garment.
And garments that were all-in-one, that you had great difficulty unbuttoning when necessary, [LAUGHS] because it buttoned up across the back.
So you had quite a problem with this.
And I remember I had a rain cape, which nobody-- no children in England had, and it used to embarrass me.
It was a cape with a hood.
And the usual absolute necessities were in that case.
Only the necessities, because that's all you could carry.
And I have a list of this, which perhaps will be shown on camera, I don't know.
And there were several things that were confiscated from that list.
There was just some few little bits of pathetic jewelry were taken away from us.
Apart from that, that's all.
And I suppose I had my doll.
I'm sure I must have had a doll, because I loved my toys.
But I can't remember any more.
Do you know this has gone completely from my memory.
All I can remember is them taking me and being at the station.
I can't-- I can't remember.
It's I suppose an extraordinary thing, but I don't remember it.
Maybe it's because I didn't want to remember it, I don't know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
In England, in London.
We arrived and we were taken to a hall.
I don't know where it was this hall.
And I think there were 100 children on the transport.
We awaited our the people that were collecting us.
And eventually this lady turned up, who I-- we were introduced to.
And I called her Auntie Ann.
She was one of four sisters.
One was married and the other three were unmarried, and all lived together in a very nice house in Hampstead Garden suburb, where we were taken.
Auntie Ann was a very strange lady because she had mental problems, which because I didn't know.
And she gave us a hard time.
I remember several incidents and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which were not terribly pleasant.
But on the whole, I mean, they looked after us.
She had no idea, she was a spinster.
And so she had really no idea how to look, how to treat, two little foreign children.
We weren't allowed to speak any German, although we couldn't-- I'm sorry, we weren't allowed to speak any English, although we couldn't speak English.
So we probably whispered amongst ourselves, but we weren't allowed to speak any German.
She wanted us to learn English, which we learned actually very quickly.
I think, within six months, I could speak English.
